question: A current of $10~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Ag}^{1+}$ ions for $38~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Ag}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Ag} = 107.9~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 22797.33~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.2363~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $1~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.2363}{1} = 0.2363~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.2363)(107.9) \approx 25.49~\text{g}$.

question: In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $297~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $393~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.
Calculate the mass of $0.78~\text{moles}$ of $\text{C}_{5}\text{H}_{12}$. (Molar mass = $72.17~\text{g/mol}$)
A reaction consumes $23.3~\text{g}$ of reactant A ($M = 124~\text{g/mol}$) to produce product B ($M = 41~\text{g/mol}$) in a $1:1$ molar ratio. Calculate the theoretical yield of product B in grams.
explanation: Moles of A $n = \frac{23.3}{124} = 0.19~\text{mol}$. Since ratio is $1:1$, moles of B $= 0.19$. Mass of B $m = n \times M_B = (0.19)(41) = 7.7~\text{g}$.

question: Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $2517.24~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
The electronegativity difference between Atom P and Atom Q is $1.4$. If Atom P is the more electronegative atom with an electronegativity of $5.2$, what is the electronegativity of Atom Q?
A metal has a work function of $2.75\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate its threshold frequency. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

A current of $3.76~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions for $45~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Cu}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Cu} = 63.5~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 10144.99~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.1051~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $2~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.1051}{2} = 0.0526~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0526)(63.5) \approx 3.34~\text{g}$.

question: A $1.69~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $20~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.69}{40.0} = 0.0423~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0423$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0423}{0.02} = 2.11~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: In a titration, $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.05~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $21.73~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.

A current of $6.26~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Ag}^{1+}$ ions for $24~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Ag}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Ag} = 107.9~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 9019.23~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.0935~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $1~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.0935}{1} = 0.0935~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0935)(107.9) \approx 10.09~\text{g}$.

question: A sample of a radioactive substance initially contains $839~\text{g}$. After $28~\text{days}$, $52.44~\text{g}$ remains. Calculate the half-life of the substance.
In a reaction, $61~\text{cm}^3$ of hydrogen gas is produced in $33~\text{s}$. Calculate the rate of production of hydrogen gas.

For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.2~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.6~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 0.6~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.

Light of frequency $1.30\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.85\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.95~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.64~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 1.61~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.

For the reaction $3\text{A} + 2\text{B} \rightarrow \text{Products}$, you have $1.54~\text{mol}$ of A and $3.51~\text{mol}$ of B. Calculate the moles of the excess reactant remaining after the reaction goes to completion.
explanation: Limiting is A. Moles of B used = $\frac{2}{3} \times 1.54 = 1.03~\text{mol}$. Remaining = $3.51 - 1.03 = 2.48~\text{mol}$.

question: A $1.97~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.97}{40.0} = 0.0492~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0492$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0492}{0.01} = 4.92~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: Light of frequency $1.33\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.79\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)
In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $216~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $575~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.

Light of frequency $1.23\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.36\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)
A reaction produces $\text{CO}_2$ gas ($M = 44.0~\text{g/mol}$). If $0.045~\text{moles}$ of $\text{CO}_2$ are produced in $60~\text{s}$, calculate the rate of reaction in $\text{g/s}$.
explanation: Mass of $\text{CO}_2$ = $n \times M = (0.045)(44.0) = 1.98~\text{g}$. Rate = $\frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.98}{60} = 0.033~\text{g/s}$.

question: Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $11091.2~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $426~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $450~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.

Light of frequency $1.48\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.25\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

A current of $3.6~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ ions for $60~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Zn}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Zn} = 65.4~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 12943.18~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.1341~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $2~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.1341}{2} = 0.0671~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0671)(65.4) \approx 4.39~\text{g}$.

question: For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$, equilibrium concentrations are: $[\text{N}_2] = 0.1~\text{M}$, $[\text{H}_2] = 0.44~\text{M}$, $[\text{NH}_3] = 0.57~\text{M}$. Calculate $K_c$.
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $14183.51~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $13964.18~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

In a titration, $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.05~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $27.91~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.

A current of $3.13~\text{A}$ is passed through a solution containing $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions for $29~\text{minutes}$. Calculate the mass of $\text{Al}$ deposited at the cathode. ($M_\text{Al} = 27.0~\text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
explanation: $Q = It = 5438.05~\text{C}$. $n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = 0.0564~\text{mol}$. The ion requires $3~\text{mol}$ of electrons per mole of metal, so $n_{\text{metal}} = \frac{0.0564}{3} = 0.0188~\text{mol}$. Mass = $n \times M = (0.0188)(27.0) \approx 0.51~\text{g}$.

question: The electronegativity difference between Atom P and Atom Q is $1.1$. If Atom P is the more electronegative atom with an electronegativity of $5.1$, what is the electronegativity of Atom Q?
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $4738.02~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)
Calculate the number of moles of electrons transferred when a charge of $6749.61~\text{C}$ passes through a cell. (Faraday's constant $F = 96485~\text{C/mol}$)

Light of frequency $1.03\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.71\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

A reaction consumes $259.35~\text{g}$ of reactant A ($M = 99~\text{g/mol}$) to produce product B ($M = 166~\text{g/mol}$) in a $1:1$ molar ratio. Calculate the theoretical yield of product B in grams.
explanation: Moles of A $n = \frac{259.35}{99} = 2.62~\text{mol}$. Since ratio is $1:1$, moles of B $= 2.62$. Mass of B $m = n \times M_B = (2.62)(166) = 434.87~\text{g}$.

question: A reaction has $\Delta H = -206~\text{kJ/mol}$. The total energy released by forming new bonds is $661~\text{kJ/mol}$. The reactants contain $1~\text{mol}$ of A-A bonds ($E = 127~\text{kJ/mol}$) and $2~\text{mol}$ of B-B bonds. Calculate the bond energy of the B-B bond.

A reaction has $\Delta H = -388~\text{kJ/mol}$. The total energy released by forming new bonds is $601~\text{kJ/mol}$. The reactants contain $1~\text{mol}$ of A-A bonds ($E = 102~\text{kJ/mol}$) and $2~\text{mol}$ of B-B bonds. Calculate the bond energy of the B-B bond.

A $1.33~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.33}{40.0} = 0.0333~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0333$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0333}{0.01} = 3.33~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

question: In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $413~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $504~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.
In a reaction, $95~\text{cm}^3$ of hydrogen gas is produced in $35~\text{s}$. Calculate the rate of production of hydrogen gas.
In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $385~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $469~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.
A solution has a pH of $9.4$. Calculate the hydrogen ion concentration, $[\text{H}^+]$.

For the reaction $2\text{A} + 2\text{B} \rightarrow \text{Products}$, you have $1.11~\text{mol}$ of A and $3.48~\text{mol}$ of B. Calculate the moles of the excess reactant remaining after the reaction goes to completion.
explanation: Limiting is A. Moles of B used = $\frac{2}{2} \times 1.11 = 1.11~\text{mol}$. Remaining = $3.48 - 1.11 = 2.37~\text{mol}$.

question: In a chemical reaction, the sum of bond energies for bonds broken is $323~\text{kJ/mol}$ and for bonds formed is $671~\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction.
A reaction consumes $16.43~\text{g}$ of reactant A ($M = 41~\text{g/mol}$) to produce product B ($M = 178~\text{g/mol}$) in a $1:1$ molar ratio. Calculate the theoretical yield of product B in grams.
explanation: Moles of A $n = \frac{16.43}{41} = 0.4~\text{mol}$. Since ratio is $1:1$, moles of B $= 0.4$. Mass of B $m = n \times M_B = (0.4)(178) = 71.35~\text{g}$.

question: A metal has a work function of $3.83\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate its threshold frequency. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)

Light of frequency $1.39\times 10^{+15}~\text{Hz}$ shines on a metal with a work function of $3.62\times 10^{-19}~\text{J}$. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}~\text{J\cdot s}$)
Calculate the mass of $2.62~\text{moles}$ of $\text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{6}\text{O}_{2}$. (Molar mass = $62.08~\text{g/mol}$)

A $1.29~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $25~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.29}{40.0} = 0.0323~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0323$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0323}{0.025} = 1.29~\text{mol/dm}^3$.
